answer: The matla of ghazal 70 in Diwan-E-Ghalib (mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c, chapter 70) reads: 'Hazaaron khwahishein aisi ke har khwahish pe dam nikle / Bohat nikle mere armaan lekin phir bhi kam nikle.' On the question of each desire being capable of taking a life: the phrase 'har khwahish pe dam nikle' means 'upon each desire, the breath (life) departs.' 'Dam' means breath or life-force, and 'nikle' means departs/leaves. So the matla declares that the speaker has thousands of desires, each of such overwhelming intensity that experiencing or longing for it causes one's very breath — one's life — to leave the body. Every single desire, in its fervor, is lethal enough to kill. The second hemistich adds bitter irony: many of those desires were indeed fulfilled (bohat nikle mere armaan), yet they still felt too few (phir bhi kam nikle) — the longing is so inexhaustible that even fulfilled desires leave the lover bereft.

tool_calls.result_summary: Translation confirms matla: 'A thousand wishes, each could make my spirit cease; / So many of my dreams came true, yet brought so little peace.'
